---
bibliography: "quantificational-logic.bib"
number-sections: true
crossref:
  chapters: true
---

{{< include /assets/macros/macros.qmd >}}

# Quantificational Logic

{{< include introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include syntax.qmd >}}

:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-10"}

## The Semantics of Quantificational Logic

### The Significance of the Quantifiers

Having introduced the formal language L$_Q$, we must now specify a semantics for
this language which will allow us to determine which arguments are valid. As
usual, such a semantics must tell us how to intepret the non-logical elements of
the language. In the case of L$_Q$, the non-logical elements are the constants
and the predicates. How should we interpret these items?

Since constants are meant to function as names of individual objects, our
interpretations must take place against the background of a fixed set, or
domain, of objects. An interpretation must assign to each name an object in this
set. What about the predicates? Unary predicates are meant to denote properties
of objects, and, so following the extensional approach discuss in Chapter 1, we
can interpret a unary predicate as a set of objects in the domain. This set of
objects signifies the set of all objects possessing the property named by the
predicate. What about binary predicates?

Binary predicates are meant to stand for two-place relations. We can represent
such relations not by a set of objects in the domain, but instead by a set of
_ordered pairs_ of objects taken from the domain. The pairs in this set stand
for those pairs of objects the first member of which stands in said relation to
the second. If we wish to represent such a set of order pairs diagramatically,
we can do by means of a collection of directed edges, or arrows. For example:

:::{#fig-binary-relation}
![](/content/static/images/binary-relation.svg){width=200 fig-align="center"
fig-alt="Binary Relation"}

Diagrammatic depiction of a two-place relation
:::

In this diagram, an ordered pair belongs to the extension of the relation
depicted just in case an arrow points from the first member of the pair to the
second. So, in set theoretic notation, the diagram represents the two-place
relation whose extension is given by the set:

$$\{(a, b), (b, c), (c, b), (c, c), (b, d) \}$$

What about a ternary predicate symbol? Following along with the above idea, a
ternary predicate symbol---denoting a three-place relation---will be represented
by a set of ordered _triples_ of objects taken from the domain. Three-place (or
more) relations are a bit harder to depict diagrammatically, so it's better to
just use the set notation. The same approach can be extended so as to apply to
predicates of higher arity. In general, an $n$-ary predicate will be interpreted
as a set of ordered $n$-tuples of objects taken from the domain.

All told, then, we have the following definition of an interpretation of the
language L$_Q$:

:::{#def-interpretation-Lq .definition}
Take as given a fixed set of objects, $\W$, referred to as the domain. An interpretation of
the language is a function $\si$ such that:

1. For each constant $a$, $\si(a)\in \W$ (we will assume that, for every $x\in
   \W$, $\si(a)=x$, for some constant $a$).

2. For each predicate $P^1$, $\si(P)\subset \W$.

3. For each predicate $P^n$, $\si(P)$ is a set of ordered $n$-tuples of objects
   in $\W$, i.e.:

   $$\si(P)\subset\{(a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n): a_i\in \W\textrm{, for }i=1,\ldots,n\} $$

:::

Now, that we know what an interpretation is, our semantics must tell us how to
assess the truth or falsehood of sentences in a given interpretation. In
specifying how we go from an intepretation to a truth valuation, we will have
provided an account of the intended meanings of the logical elements of the
syntax, most notably, the quantifiers.

The simplest sentences in the language are those of the form:

$$ Pa_1 a_2\cdots a_n$$

where $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol and $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ are constants. In
this case, the sentence should be true just in case the objects named by the
constants stand in the specific $n$-place relation named by the predicate. This
semantic rule for such elementary sentences can be summarized as follows. For
any interpretation $\si$:

$$
v^\si(Pa_1 a_2\cdots a_n) = \true\textrm{\quad iff \quad}
(\si(a_1),\si(a_2),\ldots,\si(a_n))\in \si(P)
$$

The propositional connectives will be given the same truth-functional meaning as
was ascribed to them in Chapter 2. In particular, the truth values of
propositional compounds will be determined by the truth values of their
component sentences in accordance with the following semantic rules:

$$
v^\si(\neg\ph) = \true \textrm{\quad iff \quad} v^\si(\ph)=\false
$$

$$
v^\si(\ph\conj\psi) = \true \textrm{\quad iff \quad} v^\si(\ph)=\true\textrm{
and }v^\si(\psi)=\true
$$

$$
v^\si(\ph\disj\psi) = \true \textrm{\quad iff \quad} v^\si(\ph)=\true\textrm{
or }v^\si(\psi)=\true \textrm{ (or both)}
$$

$$
v^\si(\ph\imp\psi) = \false \textrm{\quad iff \quad} v^\si(\ph)=\true\textrm{
and }v^\si(\psi)=\false
$$

$$
v^\si(\ph\iff\psi) = \true \textrm{\quad iff \quad} v^\si(\ph)=v^\si(\psi)
$$

Lastly, we must explain how to assess the truth values of quantified sentences
in a given interpretation. To do so, we must introduce one further piece of
terminology. Let $\ph$ be a formula, such as:

$$\forall z(Rxz\rightarrow Py)$$

In this formula, the only variables that occur freely are the variables $x$ and $y$. To
indicate this fact, we denote the formula as follows:

$$ \ph(x,y) $$

Note that this not a new formula. It is just another name for the formula given
above, but one which carries with it additional information about which
variables occur freely in it. In particular, this way of denoting the formula
indicates that no variables other than $x$ and $y$ occur freely in $\ph$. As
discussed above, the formula $\ph(x,y)$, having two free variables, can be
thought of as a defined two-place relation. How do I assert that this relation
holds of the objects named by the constants $a$ and $b$? I do so by substituting
$a$ for every free occurrence of $x$ in the formula, and substituting $b$ for
every free occurrence of $y$. This results in the sentence:

$$\forall z(Raz\rightarrow Pb)$$

which we will denote by:

$$ \ph(a,b) $$

We will term this process, whereby a formula is turned
into a sentence by replacing the free occurrences of variables in it with
constants, _instantiation_.

With this idea in hand, we can now can return to the question of how to assess
the truth or falsehood of quantified sentences. Consider the sentence:

$$\forall x(\ph)$$

Note that this may be a sentence despite the fact that the formula $\ph$
appearing in the scope of the quantifier is not itself a sentence. But if so,
then it must be the case that the _only_ variable occurring freely in the
formula $\ph$ is $x$, and so we can denote $\ph$ by $\ph(x)$. The universal
quantifier means that _every_ instantiation of this formula, that is every
sentence of the forma $\ph(a)$, expresses a true
sentence. We can capture this fact, by the following semantic rule:

$$
v^\si(\forall x(\ph))=\true\textrm{\quad iff \quad}
v^\si(\ph(a))=\true\textrm{, for every constant $a$.}
$$

In similar fashion, the existential quantifier can be interpreted as meaning
that _some_ instantiation of the formula to which the quantifier applies is a true sentence.

$$
v^\si(\exists x(\ph))=\true\textrm{\quad iff \quad}
v^\si(\ph(a))=\true\textrm{, for some constant $a$.}
$$

This completes our semantic specification of the truth conditions for the
sentences of L$_Q$. Let's see how this semantics allows us to assess the
validity of arguments. Consider the argument introduced in the previous section:

$$
\forall x(\exists y(Pyx\wedge Ayx)\rightarrow Tx), Paj\proves Aaj\rightarrow Tj
$$

Suppose that the first premise is true in some interpretation $\si$, i.e.:

$$ v^\si(\forall x(\exists y(Pyx\wedge Ayx)\rightarrow Tx))=\true$$

In accordance with our above rule, this means that every instantiation of the
formula:

$$\exists y(Pyx\wedge Ayx)\rightarrow Tx$$

in which $x$ occurs freely, is true in the intepretation $\si$. In particular:

$$
v^\si(\exists y(Pyj\wedge Ayj)\rightarrow Tj)=\true
$$

The second premise says that $Paj$ is true in $\si$. To show that the truth of
the conclusion follows, let's suppose that $Aaj$ is true and try to argue for the
truth of $Tj$. If $Aaj$ is true, then:

$$ v^\si(Paj\wedge Aaj)=\true $$

Now, by our rule for existentially quantified sentencs, it follows that:

$$ v^\si(\exists x (Pxj\wedge Axj))=\true $$

But since we have already seen that the conditional $\exists y(Pyj\wedge
Ayj)\rightarrow Tj$ is true, it follows that $Tj$ is true as well. So the truth
of the conclusion follows from the truth of the premises, i.e., the argument is
valid.

Let's now consider an invalid argument:

$$ \forall x(\exists y (Rxy))\proves \exists x(\forall y (Rxy)) $$

To show that this argument is invalid, we must provide an interpretation under
which the premise is true and the conclusion false. The premise says that
everything stands in the relation $R$ to something. The conclusion says that
something stands in the relation $R$ to everything. One interpretation of the
relation $R$ on which the first of these claims is true and the second false is
the following:

![](/content/static/images/binary-relation-2.svg){width=160 fig-align="center"
fig-alt="Binary Relation"}

In this diagram, every object points to something, but no object points to
everything. In particular $a$ does not point to itself and neither does $b$. So,
the premise is true but the conclusion is false. The argument is invalid.

:::{#exr-countermodels .exercise}

{{< include /content/exercises/countermodels.qmd >}}

:::

:::

:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-10"}
:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-11"}

### Orders, Equivalences and Identity

Consider the following argument:

$$
\frac{\textrm{Sue is taller than James\hspace{3mm},\hspace{3mm} Sue is not
taller than Bob}}{\textrm{James is not taller than Bob}}
$$

Intuitively, this seems like a good argument, for the truth of the premises does
seem to entail the truth of the conclusion. But suppose we formalize this
argument in L$_Q$ by introducing constants naming Sue ($s$), James ($j$), and
Bob ($b$), and a binary predicate $T$ that stands for the two-place relation
'... is taller than ---'. The argument would then be rendered as follows:

$$
Tsj, \neg Tsb\proves \neg Tjb
$$

But this is _not_ a valid argument, for it is easy to give an interpretation of
the language in which the premises are true and the conclusion false. All that
is required is to interpret $T$ such that its extension includes the ordered
pairs $(\textrm{Sue}, \textrm{James})$ and $(\textrm{James}, \textrm{Bob})$, but does not include the ordered pair
$(\textrm{Sue}, \textrm{Bob})$.

Why then does the argument appear to be valid? The answer is that the relation
'... is taller than ---' is not just any relatio. It is a special kind of
relation. It does not just relate arbitrary individuals, but it relates
individuals by _ordering_ them according to their height. We call relations like
'... is taller than ---' ordering relations, or simply _orders_, for short. In
addition to '... is taller than ---', there are lots of other orders as well. The
relation '... is older than ---' is an order (by age), and the relation '... is to
the left of ---' is an order (by position), and the relation '... is a proper subset of
---' is an order (by set-theoretic content).

To recognize the above argument as valid, we need to include among our
assumptions the additional posit that the two place relation '... is taller than
---' is an order. How do we do so? It turns out that orders have certain special
features that not all relations possess and which single them out as orders. In
particular, an order is any two place relation $R$ which satisfies the following
two properties:

Irreflexivity: Nothing stands in the relation $R$ to itself.

Transitivity: If $x$ stands in the relation $R$ to $y$, and $y$ stands in the
relation $R$ to $z$, then $x$ stands in the relation $R$ to $z$.

To see that '... is taller than---' is an order, it is enough to note that it is
an irreflexive, transitive relation. After all, nobody is taller
than themself, and if one person is taller than another, and that other person is
taller than a third, then the first person is taller than the third. Note that
both the conditions of irreflexivity and transitivity can be expressed in the
language L$_Q$. Applied to the binary predicate $T$, we can state these
conditions as follows:

Irreflexivity: $\forall x(\neg Txx)$

Transitivity: $\forall x(\forall y(\forall z(Txy \wedge Tyz \rightarrow Txz)))$

To stipulate that $T$ is an order, we simply add
these two sentences to the argument as premises:

$$
\forall x(\neg Txx), \forall x(\forall y(\forall z(Txy \wedge Tyz \rightarrow
Txz))), Tsj, \neg Tsb\proves \neg Tjb
$$

This argument is valid. To see why, suppose for contradiction that the
premises are all true, but that the conclusion is false. If the conclusion is
false it means that $Tjb$ is true. Now, $Tsj$ is true as well. Since the
transitivity condition is also true, by the truth conditions for universally
quantified sentences, we have that the sentence:

$$Tsj\wedge Tjb\rightarrow Tsb$$

Hence, $Tsb$ is true, which contradicts the assumption that the premise $\neg
Tsb$ is true. Hence, the argument is valid.

Exercise:

Here's another example of a valid argument that relies on the ordering
assumption:

$$
\frac{\textrm{Sue is taller than James}}{\textrm{James is not taller than Sue}}
$$

Equivalences:

$$
\frac{\textrm{Sue is in the same grade as James\hspace{3mm},\hspace{3mm} Bob
is in the same grade as James}}{\textrm{Sue is in the same grade than Bob}}
$$

Again, this is not a valid argument as formulated. But the relation '... is in
the same grade as ---' is not an order, since it is reflexive. Instead it is
another kind of special relation, one which relates things by grouping them
together in accordance with some shared property. These relations are called
equivalence relations, or _equivalences_, for short.

Reflexivity: $\forall x(\neg Gxx)$

Symmetry: $\forall x(\forall y(Gxy\imp Gyx))$

Transitivity: $\forall x(\forall y(\forall z(Gxy \wedge Gyz \rightarrow Gxz)))$

Exercise:

$P(x)\iff P(y)$

There is one special equivalence relation that is so fundamental to our
reasoning, that it is often treated as part of the logical syntax of the
language. This is the relation '... is identical to ---', or the so-called
_identity_ relation. It is the relation that obtains between any object and
itself, but fails to obtain between any object and any other. Because it so
fundamental, we often give this relation a special name in the language, `='. In
the case of this one predicate, it is also standard to use the infix instead the prefix
notation. Thus, we write $x = y$ in place of $=xy$. Also, for the formula $\neg x
= y$, we adopt the shorthand $x\neq y$.

When we say that identity is sometimes treated as a logical notion, what we mean
is that its meaning is fixed across all interpretations. In particular, we
stipulate that, in any interpretation, the extension of the binary predicate '$=$'
is just:

$$\{(a,a): a\in \W\}$$

There is exactly one object in the universe with property $P$. There are exactly
two objects in the universe with property $P$.

$L^2$: '... is to the left of ---'
$R^2$: '... is to the right of ---'

We will assume that $L$ and $R$ are orders and that:

$$ \forall x(\forall y((x\neq y)\imp (Lxy\iff\neg Rxy)))$$

Define the following properties and relations:

$B^3$: '... is between --- and \_\_\_'

$R^1$: '... is right-most'

$L^1$: '... is left-most'

$I$: '... is immediately to the right of ---'

Write down sentences expressing the following claims:

There is someone two places to the right of John.

There are exactly two people between John and Amy.

John is one person away from the left of the line.

:::
:::

## References {.unnumbered}
